subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — Middle of Year (MOY) Test 1
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions that follow. Some questions ask you to use both parts of the text. For each question, choose the best answer or answers based on what the selection states or suggests. You have 90 minutes.

passages.title: The Quilt That Talked
passages.text:
Part 1

Grandma Pearl kept an old quilt folded at the foot of her bed. To Mia, it looked plain, just rows of faded cloth squares stitched together. "Why do you keep this raggedy thing?" Mia asked one rainy afternoon.

Grandma Pearl smiled and spread the quilt across her lap. "This quilt talks," she said. "You only have to learn how to listen." Mia frowned, because quilts cannot talk. But she sat down beside her grandmother anyway.

Grandma Pearl pointed to a blue square. "This piece came from your grandfather's work shirt. He wore it every day at the railroad." Then she touched a square covered in tiny yellow flowers. "This was my Sunday dress when I was a girl your age. I wore it the day I learned to read."

Square by square, the quilt told its stories. A green patch was once a tablecloth from a family supper. A soft white square had been a baby blanket — Mia's own, when she was small. Mia ran her fingers over the white cloth. It did not feel raggedy anymore. It felt warm, as if it remembered being wrapped around her.

Part 2

"Now you try," Grandma Pearl said. "Find a square and tell me what it says."

Mia looked closely. She chose a red square with a small tear near one corner. "I don't know this one," she admitted.

"That," said Grandma Pearl, "is the most important square of all. It came from the apron I was wearing the day your mother was born. The tear is from when I caught it on the hospital door, running too fast to meet her."

Mia laughed and pressed the red square to her cheek. Suddenly the quilt did not look plain. Every square was a window into a day that had already happened, kept safe in thread.

That night, Grandma Pearl tucked the quilt around Mia's shoulders. "Listen," she whispered. And though the room was quiet, Mia was sure she could hear the squares humming the old, soft stories — railroad mornings, Sunday lessons, and the squeak of a hospital door swinging open. She fell asleep before the quilt was finished talking.

passages.title: How Honeybees Talk With a Dance
passages.text:
Part 1

Honeybees cannot speak the way people do, yet they share important news every day. When a bee finds a patch of flowers full of sweet nectar, it must tell the other bees in the hive where to go. To do this, the bee does a special dance.

The most famous dance is called the waggle dance. The bee runs forward in a straight line while shaking, or waggling, its body from side to side. Then it loops back and runs the line again, tracing a path shaped like the number eight. This dance may look like wiggling, but it is really a set of directions.

The direction of the waggle run tells the other bees which way to fly. If the bee waggles straight up, the flowers are toward the sun. If it waggles to the left, the food is to the left of the sun. In this way, one small bee can point the whole hive in the right direction.

Part 2

The dance also tells the bees how far to travel. A longer waggle run means the flowers are far away. A shorter run means they are close to the hive. Bees that watch the dance can learn both the direction and the distance without ever leaving home.

Why does this matter? A hive may hold thousands of bees. If every bee had to search alone, they would waste time and energy flying in circles. Because of the waggle dance, the bees can work together. One scout finds the food, returns to the hive, and shares the news. Soon many bees fly straight to the flowers and bring back nectar to make honey.

Scientists once thought the dancing was just an accident. Then they marked single bees with tiny dots of paint and followed them. They proved that bees who watched the dance flew to the exact spot the dancer described. The honeybee's dance is one of the clearest examples of animals sharing directions, and it helps the whole hive survive.

passages.title: The Pony Express: Mail by Horse
passages.text:
Part 1

Long before phones or email, getting a letter across the United States was slow and hard. In 1860, a message sent from Missouri to California could take weeks to arrive by wagon or boat. Some people felt this was far too slow for a growing country. They wanted a faster way to carry the mail across the wide land in the West.

Three businessmen had an idea. They would use fast horses and brave riders to carry the mail in a relay, passing it from one rider to the next. They called their plan the Pony Express. The riders would race day and night across nearly two thousand miles, through deserts, mountains, and rivers.

The Pony Express built small stations along the trail. At each station, a tired horse was swapped for a fresh one in about two minutes. A rider would gallop fifty miles or more before handing the mail to the next rider, who carried it on. Because the horses were always fresh, the mail almost never stopped moving.

Part 2

The riders faced great danger. They rode through blazing heat and freezing snow. Storms could hide the trail, and the work was lonely. To travel light and fast, riders carried little more than the mail and a small horn to signal the next station.

The Pony Express was a success at its main goal. It cut the delivery time from weeks to only about ten days. News that once crawled across the country now seemed to fly. People in California finally felt closer to the rest of the nation.

Yet the Pony Express did not last long. In late 1861, workers finished a telegraph line that could send messages across the country in seconds using electric signals over wires. A horse, no matter how fast, could not beat the speed of the telegraph. Just eighteen months after it began, the Pony Express closed for good. Even so, it is still remembered as a bold chapter in the story of how Americans worked to connect their country.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, where did the blue square in the quilt come from?
options: A tablecloth from a family supper
Grandma Pearl's Sunday dress
Grandfather's work shirt
Mia's baby blanket
questions.explanation: In Part 1, Grandma Pearl points to a blue square and says, "This piece came from your grandfather's work shirt."

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "raggedy" as it is used in Part 1?
options: Bright and new
Soft and warm
Worn and shabby
Large and heavy
questions.explanation: Mia first sees the quilt as plain and "raggedy," meaning old and worn. Later the white square "did not feel raggedy anymore" but "felt warm," showing raggedy is the opposite of warm and new.

questions.questionText: Which word BEST describes how Mia's feelings change from the beginning to the end of the story?
options: From afraid to bored
From doubtful to delighted
From cheerful to angry
From proud to ashamed
questions.explanation: Mia begins by frowning and saying quilts cannot talk (doubtful), but by the end she laughs, presses a square to her cheek, and falls asleep happily listening to the stories (delighted).

questions.questionText: Read this sentence from Part 2: "Every square was a window into a day that had already happened." What is the meaning of the phrase "a window into a day"?
options: An opening that keeps out the cold air
A spot that needs to be cleaned often
A piece of glass that breaks easily
A way to look back at a moment from the past
questions.explanation: A window is something you look through to see. The phrase means each square lets Mia look back at and remember a day from the past, not a real glass window.

questions.questionText: What is the main lesson, or theme, of this story?
options: Old quilts should be thrown away when they tear.
Ordinary objects can hold meaningful family memories.
Children should not ask their grandparents questions.
Sewing is the most important skill to learn.
questions.explanation: The quilt looks plain, but each square holds a family memory, and Mia learns to value it. The theme is that ordinary objects can carry meaningful memories.

questions.questionText: Which statement from the text BEST supports the idea that Mia's feelings about the quilt have changed?
options: "Grandma Pearl kept an old quilt folded at the foot of her bed."
"Square by square, the quilt told its stories."
"It did not feel raggedy anymore. It felt warm, as if it remembered being wrapped around her."
"Mia frowned, because quilts cannot talk."
questions.explanation: The quote showing the cloth no longer felt raggedy but warm directly shows Mia's change in feeling. The other options describe the quilt or her early doubt, not her change.

questions.questionText: Why does Grandma Pearl call the red square "the most important square of all"?
options: It is the only square without a tear in it.
It is the brightest color in the whole quilt.
It came from the baby blanket that was once Mia's.
It is from the apron she wore the day Mia's mother was born.
questions.explanation: Grandma Pearl explains the red square came from the apron she wore the day Mia's mother was born, and the tear is from rushing to meet her. That memory makes it the most important.

questions.questionText: Select the TWO details from the text that show the quilt is connected to important days in the family's life.
options: The white square had been Mia's own baby blanket.
Grandma Pearl kept the quilt folded at the foot of her bed.
The room was quiet that night while Mia listened.
The yellow-flowered square was the dress Grandma Pearl wore the day she learned to read.
questions.explanation: The yellow square (the day she learned to read) and the white square (Mia's baby blanket) both mark important days. Keeping the quilt folded and the quiet room are not tied to special family days.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "nectar" as it is used in Part 1?
options: A sweet liquid found in flowers
The home where bees live
A type of dance bees perform
A small dot of paint
questions.explanation: The text says a bee finds "a patch of flowers full of sweet nectar" and that bees bring nectar back "to make honey," showing nectar is a sweet liquid in flowers.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what does the direction of the waggle run tell other bees?
options: How many bees live in the hive
How much honey is left in the hive
Which way to fly to reach the flowers
What color the flowers are
questions.explanation: Part 1 states, "The direction of the waggle run tells the other bees which way to fly."

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Flowers grow best when the sun is shining.
Honeybees fly faster than any other insect.
Scientists like to paint dots on insects for fun.
Honeybees use a special dance to share where food is located.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains how bees use the waggle dance to tell other bees the direction and distance of food. That is the main idea.

questions.questionText: Based on the text, what does a LONGER waggle run tell the other bees?
options: The flowers are far away from the hive.
The flowers are directly toward the sun.
There is no food worth visiting.
The flowers are very close to the hive.
questions.explanation: Part 2 states, "A longer waggle run means the flowers are far away. A shorter run means they are close to the hive."

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: Part 1 lists problems, and Part 2 lists the same problems again.
Part 1 describes bees, and Part 2 describes a different animal.
Part 1 explains how the dance shows direction, and Part 2 explains how it also shows distance.
Part 1 tells a story, and Part 2 explains why the story is not true.
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains how the waggle run shows direction; Part 2 adds how the same dance shows distance and why it helps the hive. The parts build on each other to fully explain the dance.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between the information about scientists in the last paragraph and the rest of the text?
options: It shows that the dance is only a lucky guess the bees make.
It describes a different way that bees make honey.
It explains how scientists teach bees to dance.
It gives proof that the dance really does share directions, as the text explains.
questions.explanation: The text explains the dance shares directions; the scientists' experiment (marking bees and following them) proved the bees flew to the exact spot, which supports the rest of the text.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text BEST supports the idea that the waggle dance helps the whole hive work together?
options: "Because of the waggle dance, the bees can work together."
"The most famous dance is called the waggle dance."
"A longer waggle run means the flowers are far away."
"Honeybees cannot speak the way people do."
questions.explanation: The sentence stating the bees "can work together" because of the dance directly supports the idea of teamwork. The others define the dance or give facts that do not mention working together.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. What must happen FIRST before many bees can fly straight to the flowers?
options: The flowers must move closer to the hive.
Scientists must mark the bees with paint.
The hive must grow to hold thousands of bees.
A scout bee finds the food and dances to share where it is.
questions.explanation: Across both parts, the order is: a scout finds the food, returns, and dances; then the watching bees learn the direction and distance and fly straight to the flowers. The scout's discovery and dance come first.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "relay" as it is used in Part 1?
options: Resting for two minutes at a station
Riding a horse in a large circle
Sending a message by electric wire
Passing something from one person to the next in turn
questions.explanation: The text says riders would "carry the mail in a relay, passing it from one rider to the next," which defines relay as passing along in turn.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, why was a fresh horse swapped in at each station?
options: So the mail would almost never stop moving
So the riders would not get lost in storms
So the horses could carry heavier loads
So the riders could rest for the night
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that fresh horses were always ready, so "the mail almost never stopped moving."

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Horses are stronger than any machine ever built.
Riders enjoyed the lonely life on the trail.
The Pony Express was a fast but short-lived way to carry mail across the country.
California was the hardest place to deliver mail.
questions.explanation: The text describes how the Pony Express sped up mail delivery but lasted only about eighteen months before the telegraph replaced it. That is the main idea.

questions.questionText: Based on the text, what was one MAIN reason the Pony Express closed for good?
options: The horses became too expensive to feed.
California stopped sending and receiving letters.
The riders no longer wanted to carry the mail.
The telegraph could send messages much faster than a horse.
questions.explanation: Part 2 states the telegraph could send messages "in seconds," and "a horse, no matter how fast, could not beat the speed of the telegraph," so the Pony Express closed.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in the last paragraph of Part 2 to the rest of the text?
options: By giving steps for how to build a station.
By telling a make-believe story about a horse.
By comparing two riders who carried the same letter.
By showing cause and effect: a faster invention caused the Pony Express to end.
questions.explanation: The earlier text shows the Pony Express was the fastest way to send mail; the last paragraph explains the telegraph arrived and was faster, which caused the Pony Express to close. This is a cause-and-effect connection.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text BEST supports the idea that the Pony Express reached its main goal?
options: "Storms could hide the trail, and the work was lonely."
"The riders faced great danger."
"It cut the delivery time from weeks to only about ten days."
"Just eighteen months after it began, the Pony Express closed for good."
questions.explanation: The main goal was faster mail. The sentence about cutting delivery time from weeks to about ten days best supports that the goal was reached. The others describe danger or its ending.

questions.questionText: Select the TWO details from the text that show the Pony Express riders faced danger on the trail.
options: Storms could hide the trail.
The riders carried a small horn to signal the next station.
A fresh horse was swapped in about two minutes.
They rode through blazing heat and freezing snow.
questions.explanation: Riding through blazing heat and freezing snow, and storms hiding the trail, both show danger. Swapping horses and carrying a horn describe how the system worked, not danger.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. What does the author MOST want the reader to understand about the Pony Express?
options: It is still the main way mail is delivered today.
It was a bold and useful idea, even though a newer invention soon replaced it.
It was a failure that never delivered any mail at all.
It was slower than the wagons and boats it replaced.
questions.explanation: Part 1 and Part 2 show the Pony Express was a bold idea that sped up mail (a success), yet it was soon replaced by the telegraph and is "still remembered as a bold chapter." The author wants readers to see it as bold and useful even though it did not last.
